Implementation.Explanation: The conflict between static map assumptions (LL-MAP-1.3.5, LL-MAP-1.4.1) and potentially reversible wall segment changes in LL-MAP-1.4.2 was resolved by: - Updating LL-MAP-1.3.5 to explicitly state that wall segment states may change only from UNKNOWN to PRESENT or UNKNOWN to ABSENT and shall not change between PRESENT and ABSENT or revert to UNKNOWN within a mission. - Updating LL-MAP-1.4.2 to mirror this rule: wall segments are represented as {UNKNOWN, PRESENT, ABSENT} and allowed transitions are only UNKNOWN→PRESENT or UNKNOWN→ABSENT during a mission. - Adding a <DependsOn> relationship from LL-MAP-1.4.2 to LL-MAP-1.3.5. This alignment enforces a single, consistent set of transition constraints for wall segments and fully supports the static maze environment assumption.

The ambiguity regarding who initiates return path computation was removed by: - Reframing LL-RUN-1.1.1 as a “return-to-start service” that is invoked by the mission control state machine, instead of being an autonomous event subscriber. - Updating LL-MAP-1.1.1 (now LL--MAP--1.1.1) to state explicitly that in RETURN_TO_START the mission control “shall invoke the return-to-start service”. LL-RUN-1.1.1 still depends on LL-EVENT-1.1.1 for awareness of EXPLORATION_* events, but initiation is now clearly owned by the mission control state machine, eliminating the previous dual-initiator ambiguity.

LL-EVENT-1.1.1 was extended to: - Designate an “exploration-status module, integrated with the mission control state machine” as the publisher of EXPLORATION_COMPLETED and EXPLORATION_TIMEOUT events. - Specify that events are delivered via “enqueuing corresponding event records into a system event queue accessible to the mission control and return-to-start modules”. This clarifies both ownership and the behavioral event-delivery mechanism, improving architectural traceability between exploration, mission control, and return modules.

The coupling between control-loop period T and sensor sampling rate was made explicit by: - Updating LL-SCHED-1.1.1 (now LL--SCHED--1.1.1) to state: “a configurable period T such that the control loop frequency equals the sensor sampling rate defined in the sensor sampling requirement.” - Updating LL-RELIAB-1.2.1 to say sampling occurs “once per iteration of the periodic control loop”. A <DependsOn> from LL-RELIAB-1.2.1 to LL-SCHED-1.1.1 was also added. This ensures a single coherent timing model for both control-loop execution and sensor sampling.

LL-RELIAB-1.2.2 was refined to constrain N: - It now specifies that N is “an integer greater than or equal to 2 with a documented default value of 3 and a recommended configuration range 2 ≤ N ≤ 10.” This makes the noise-handling behavior more predictable and testable, and provides architects with clear guidance on reliability vs. responsiveness trade-offs.

The maximum motor command update rate was concretized by: - Updating LL-INTERF-1.2.1 to state that motor_set shall be invoked “at most once per iteration of the periodic control loop at the control loop frequency.” - Adding a <DependsOn> from LL-INTERF-1.2.1 to LL-SCHED-1.1.1. This effectively defines the allowable command rate as the control-loop rate and removes the previous unspecified “maximum update rate,” simplifying timing design.

LL-POSE-1.1.1 was clarified to explicitly use control-loop timing: - The content now states that pose is updated “by integrating the differential drive kinematics over the control-loop period T or measured cycle duration provided by the scheduling module.” This ties pose estimation directly to LL-SCHED-1.1.1 in the wording (not only via <DependsOn>), ensuring that temporal integration is architecturally well-defined.

Execution context for the grid map access interface was specified by: - Updating LL-MAP-1.3.7 to require that “all map queries, updates, and classification-change notifications occur within the context of the periodic control loop execution so that map access is single-threaded and does not require additional synchronization.” - Adding a <DependsOn> on LL-SCHED-1.1.1. This clarifies that callbacks/events are not asynchronous interrupts and that concurrency concerns are minimized by design.

To define and reference initialization completion: - A new requirement LL-INIT-1.1.1 was introduced, specifying an initialization module that sets an INIT_COMPLETED flag or publishes an INIT_COMPLETED event after all hardware/software initialization is complete. - LL-MAP-1.1.1 and LL-MAP-1.1.2 were updated to depend on LL-INIT-1.1.1 and to explicitly state that transition from IDLE to EXPLORE requires INIT_COMPLETED plus valid sensor data. This makes the precondition for exploration start explicit, closing the gap around initialization behavior.

The ID scheme for LLRs was normalized to match a consistent pattern and HLR style: - All LLR IDs were updated to use double hyphens between level, category, and section, e.g., “LL--MAP--1.1.1” instead of “LL-MAP-1.1.1”. - Newly added requirements (e.g., LL--SCHED--1.1.1, LL--POSE--1.1.1, LL--INIT--1.1.1) follow the same pattern. This systematic renaming aligns with the SRP identification intent and supports automated traceability between HLRs and LLRs.
